questions.stem: These high- and low-power views of a salivary gland from a patient with Sjogren syndrome show dense focal inflammatory aggregates (arrows) replacing acini. Which cell type predominates within these infiltrates?

explanation.summary: The histologic hallmark of Sjogren syndrome is focal lymphocytic sialadenitis, in which periductal/periacinar aggregates are composed predominantly of CD4+ T cells (with B cells), quantified as a focus score on labial gland biopsy.
explanation.whyCorrect: The histologic hallmark of Sjogren syndrome is focal lymphocytic sialadenitis, in which periductal/periacinar aggregates are composed predominantly of CD4+ T cells (with B cells), quantified as a focus score on labial gland biopsy.

questions.stem: This clinical photograph shows the dry, fissured tongue of a patient with Sjogren syndrome. Which underlying change most directly accounts for this oral finding?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reduced salivary secretion from destruction of salivary acinar cells

explanation.summary: Lymphocytic destruction of salivary gland acini reduces saliva output (xerostomia), leaving the mucosa dry and fissured and predisposing to candidal overgrowth and angular cheilitis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Lymphocytic destruction of salivary gland acini reduces saliva output (xerostomia), leaving the mucosa dry and fissured and predisposing to candidal overgrowth and angular cheilitis.
